3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Made with AIIf you’ve cloned a few repos lately, you’ve probably noticed something: some have a master branch, others have main. Same Git, different names. So what's actually going on?
The Short Answer
Technically? Nothing. master and main are just branch names. Git doesn't treat one differently from the other under the hood — there's no special logic baked in for either name. The difference is entirely about convention and history, not functionality.
Where “Master” Came From
master has been Git's default branch name since Git was created in 2005. It followed a common pattern in version control systems at the time — the "master" copy was the canonical, authoritative version of the codebase.
Why “Main” Took Over
In mid-2020, GitHub switched the default branch name for new repositories from master to main. The shift was part of a broader industry move away from terminology with associations to slavery, and GitHub wasn't alone — GitLab, Bitbucket, and the Git project itself made similar moves around the same time.
It’s worth noting: this was a default change for new repos, not a forced rename of existing ones. If you made a repo before late 2020, it likely still defaults to master unless you changed it.
Does It Actually Affect Anything?
Mostly no — but there are a few practical wrinkles to know about:
1. CI/CD pipelines and scripts
If you have GitHub Actions, deployment scripts, or webhooks that explicitly reference branch: master, renaming your branch will break them until you update the references.
2. Local clones of old repos
If you clone an older repo, you’ll get master by default — your local setup just follows whatever the remote's default branch is.
3. Documentation and onboarding
Tutorials, READMEs, and onboarding docs that say “push to master” can confuse new contributors if your repo actually uses main. Worth a quick audit if you're maintaining a public project.
4. Git config defaults
Since Git 2.28, you can set your own default branch name for new repos with:
bash
git config --global init.defaultBranch mainThis doesn’t change existing repos — it just sets what git init uses going forward.
How to Rename master to main (If You Need To)
If you’ve got an existing repo on master and want to switch:
bash
# Rename locally
git branch -m master main# Push the new branch and set upstream
git push -u origin main# Update the default branch on GitHub
# (Settings → Branches → change default branch)# Delete the old branch on remote
git push origin --delete masterAfter this, make sure to:

• Update any CI/CD configs referencing master

• Notify collaborators to update their local clones (git fetch && git checkout main)

• Update README badges or links pointing to master
The Bottom Line
master vs main isn't a technical decision — it's a naming one. Git doesn't care what you call your default branch; humans (and your tooling) do. If you're starting a new project, main is the modern default and what most platforms now use out of the box. If you're maintaining an older repo, the rename is optional but increasingly standard practice.
Either way, the real lesson here is a good one for Git in general: most of what feels like “rules” in Git is actually just convention. Know the difference, and you’ll spend a lot less time confused by other people’s repos.
